content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission,
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

June 9, 1982


David H. Hirsch
City Attorney
City of Lompoc
City Hall
100 Civic Center Plaza
Lompoc, CA 93438

Re: Request for Advice
No. A-82-109

Dear Mr. Hirsch:

I apologize for any inconvenience that the delay in my response has caused you. You requested advice from this office regarding the disclosure obligations of the City's Advisory Landmark Committee under the Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections 81000, et seq.1/

My understanding of the situation from your letter and our phone discussions is as follows. The Advisory Landmark Committee was included in the City's Conflict of Interest Code by way of amendment in January 1982. When Committee members objected to filing economic disclosure statements, you advised the City Council that the Committee was not "solely advisory" within the meaning of Section 82019.2/ I concurred in your analysis.

_____________________

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted.

2/ As you discussed in your letter, the Committee's responsibilities include making temporary designations of historical landmarks; controlling the demolition, removal and alteration of designated sites; and enforcing the conditions imposed by the Ordinance.

David H. Hirsch
June 9, 1982
Page 2


     The City Council then asked you to draft an amended
version of the Advisory Landmark Committee Ordinance which
would limit the Committee to a "solely advisory" function.
The amendments to the Ordinance delete the Committee's
authority to make temporary designations and to enforce the
Ordinance.  Under the amended Ordinance, the Committee
would review applications received by the City for the
designation of historical landmarks and make recommen-
dations to the City Council based on the standards set
forth in the Ordinance.  In addition, the Committee would
review requests and make recommendations to the City
Planning Commission with regard to the demolition or
alteration of designated sites.

     As you know, Section 87302 requires that all employees
and officials who make or participate in the making of
decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on
any financial interest must be designated in a conflict of
interest code, section 82019, however, expressly excludes
from disclosure obligations unsalaried members of any board
or commission which serves a solely advisory function.

     In Commission on Cal. State Gov. Org. & Econ. v. Fair
Political Practices Com., 75 Cal. App. 3d 716, 721 (1977),
the court stated that "the word advisory denotes indirect,
relatively passive, hortatory and nonbinding counsel or
guidance, as contrasted with active management,
decisionmaking and imposition of obligatory orders or
decrees."  The court further noted that "[t]he presence or
absence of decisionmaking power is thus an important factor
in identifying the wielder of a solely advisory function."

     Pursuant to Commission regulation, a board or
commission possesses decisionmaking power or authority if
any of the following are true:

          (A)  It may make a final governmental decision;

          (B)  It may compel a governmental decision either
by reason of an exclusive power to initiate the
decision or by reason of a veto which may not be
overridden; or

David H. Hirsch
June 9, 1982
Page 3


                (C)  It makes substantive recommendations which
are, and over an extended period of time have been,
regularly approved without significant amendment or
modification by another public official or governmental
agency.

                                Commission Regulation 2 Cal.
                                Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(1).
                                (Copy enclosed.)

     The amended version of the Advisory Landmark Committee
Ordinance clearly does not confer on the Committee the
power either to make a final governmental decision or to
compel a governmental decision.  However, it is equally
clear the Committee would still be making substantive
recommendations concerning specific applications for
designation and demolition or alteration, decisions which
will affect financial interests.  Accordingly, the question
is whether these recommendations will be regularly accepted
by the City Council and Planning Commission without
significant amendment or modification.  The extent of the
Committee's influence over the final decision is essen-
tially a factual question, and it may be difficult to
determine in advance of the actual operation of the new
Ordinance.

     One possible indication now is the procedural form of
the decisionmaking.  How much time and consideration are the
Council and Planning Commission plan to devote to the
applications may be a clue.  For example, if the
application along with the Committee's recommendation are
placed on a consent calendar and no discussion or hearing
will occur unless there is an objection to the Committee's
recommendation, then the Committee has decisionmaking
authority.  On the other hand, if the Committee's role is
limited to independently reviewing the application and
making a recommendation to the Council or Planning
Commission, and the Council or Planning Commission actually
holds the hearing on the matter, receives the arguments,
and deliberates and makes its own independent decision,
then the Committee's role is probably solely advisory.
However, even under these circumstances, the facts may
indicate that the Committee's recommendations are being
routinely accepted by the Council and, it will thus not be
solely advisory.

David H. Hirsch
June 9, 1982
Page 4


     I understand that the intent of the City Council is
that the Committee is to serve a solely advisory function.
It also appears from the amended Ordinance that the
Committee's function is designed to be solely advisory.
Therefore, I see no problem in removing the Committee from
the City's Conflict of Interest Code under the
circumstances unless, in view of the above discussion, you
or the City Council conclude that the Committee will serve
in more than a purely advisory role. If the circumstances
change and it becomes clear that the Committee's role in
the designation and other decisions is more influential
than now appears to be intended, its exclusion from the
Code should be reconsidered.

     Please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901 if you
would like to discuss this letter of if I can be of further
assistance.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Diane Maura Fishburn
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

DMF:plh
Attachment

CITY OF LOMPOC
VALLEY OF FLOWERS

APR 20 8 53 AM '82

April 16, 1982


Ms. Diane Fishburne
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re: Request for Written Advice Regarding
    the Lompoc Advisory Landmark Committee.

Dear Ms. Fishburne:

    Since we have informally discussed this matter by telephone on a couple of
occasions, I am taking the liberty of addressing my request to your attention.
The City of Lompoc is requesting written advice regarding the conflict of
interest laws as they apply to the City's Advisory Landmark Committee. More
specifically, the question is whether the enclosed proposed revision of our
Ordinance governing the Committee will bring its members within the exceptions
of Government Code §82019.

    As you will recall from our conversations, this request is the result of a
situation that has developed due to the reactivation of the long dormant
Advisory Landmark Committee, coupled with our recent revision of our Conflict of
Interest regulations to include the Committee members among those required to
file form 730 disclosure statements. The Committee was originally created in
1967, but was only active for a year or so. In mid 1981 a request was received
from its former chairman to reactivate it. A search was undertaken to find persons
willing to serve on the Committee and finally by late 1981 the Committee
was reconstituted. In January 1982 the City amended its Conflict of Interest
Code procedures by adopting the FPPC's Model Code. This process included
setting forth the departments and agencies within the City required to file
disclosure statements. At that time the Advisory Landmark Committee was
included among those having to file statements. Accordingly, disclosure forms
were distributed to the Committee members. Immediately thereafter, the
Committee submitted a letter of protest to the City Council. The basis of their
protest was their perception that they were an advisory body only.

    In response to their objections, I prepared a memorandum to the City
Council in which the functions of the Committee were reviewed. This review
found that the Committee was not solely advisory. The Ordinance defining its
responsibilities provides that the Committee can make temporary designations of
historical landmarks which then must be approved, modified or disapproved by the
City Council within ninety days. Therefore, the Committee is doing more than
merely advising. More significantly, however, the Ordinance contains provisions
that grant the committee authority to control the demolition, removal and
alteration of sites once they are designated as landmarks. For example, the
Ordinance provides that the following condition may be imposed:


CITY OF LOMPOC,    CITY HALL,    100 CIVIC CENTER PLAZA,    LOMPOC, CALIFORNIA 93438    (805) 736-1261

Ms. Diane Fishburne
April 16, 1982
Page 2


"Demolition, removal or destruction, partially or entirely,
may be prohibited unless express consent in writing has first
had and obtained from the Lompoc Advisory Landmark Committee.
Such consent may impose all reasonable conditions deemed
appropriate by the Committee to accomplish the purpose of
this ordinance."

The Committee is also expressly given the responsibility for the enforce-
ment of the Ordinance and the conditions imposed thereunder and, if unable to
obtain compliance, may refer matters to the City Council or City Attorney for
appropriate action.

Thus, I concluded that under the existing procedures the Committee was
required to file the disclosure statements.

I also adviced that one option did exist. As you are aware, Government
Code Section 82019 does provide the following exception:

"Designated employee" does not include an elected state officer
or any unsalaried member of any board or commission which serves
a solely advisory function" (emphasis added)

Thus, it would seem that if the Ordinance governing the Committee was
amended so that the Committee's functions and responsibilities were "solely"
advisory, then they could be excluded from the disclosure requirement. The
City Council chose to go with this option. Therefore, I have drafted an amended
version of the Advisory Landmark Committee Ordinance, changing its role to advi-
sory only. The changes include deletion of authority to make temporary, 90 day
designations, and enforcement authority has been removed. Additionally,
requests to demolish or alter designated structures will be approved by the
Planning Commission, although the Advisory Landmark Committee will be able to
make recommendations to the Commission.

We are mindful that as a result of holding in the one appellate case
interpreting §82019, Commission on California State Government Organization v.
Fair Political Practices Commission (1977) 142 Cal. Rptr. 468, the "solely"
advisory exception is being narrowly interpreted. Therefore, it was felt that
it would be appropriate to request review by your office prior to adoption of
the new ordinance.

I might add that all of the members of the Committee have resigned pending
modification of the Ordinance so that they would not violate the Conflict of
Interest laws.

Ms. Diane Fishburne
April 16, 1982
Page 3


     Please let me thank you for your past assistance in this matter. We look
forward to receiving your office's advice as to whether the proposed ordinance
will be sufficient to satisfy the concerns of the Committee. If any additional
information is needed in order for you to review this matter please let me
know.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        David H. Hirsch
                                        City Attorney


DHH:an

Disk #4
LANDMARK1 - 4

AN ORDINANCE OF THE CITY OF LOMPOC
AMENDING ORDINANCE 804 RELATING TO
THE PRESERVATION OF HISTORICAL LANDMARKS

The City Council of the City of Lompoc does hereby ordain as follows:

SECTION 1. Ordinance 804 is amended to read:

1. Authority:

This ordinance is enacted pursuant to the power conferred upon

the City by Section 11 of Article VII of the California Constitution

to adopt and enforce local regulations not in conflict with general

laws. It is further adopted pursuant to the provisions of Section

37361 of the California Government Code and any amendments, additions

or successors thereto. Section 37361 now reads as follows:

"The legislative body may acquire property for the preservation or development of a historical landmark. It may also

acquire property for the development of recreational purposes

and facilities in connection therewith.

The legislative body may provide for places, buildings,

structures, works of art, and other objects, having a special

character or special historical or aesthetic interest or

value, special conditions or regulations for their

protection, enhancement, perpetuation or use, which may

include appropriate and reasonable control of the use or

appearance of neighboring private property within public

view, or both."

2. Purpose:

The purpose of this ordinance is to promote the general and economic welfare of the City of Lompoc by preserving and protecting those

places, sites, buildings, structures, works of art and other objects

having a special historical or aesthetic character or interest, for the

use, education and view of the general public and to remind the citizens of this city and visitors from elsewhere of the historical

background of the City of Lompoc. Pursuant to this ordinance, applications may be filed for designation of such places, sites, buildings,

structures, works of art and other objects as historical landmarks.

1

3. Advisory Landmark Committee Review and Recommendation.

There is hereby established a Lompoc Advisory Landmark Committee,

the number of members of which are to be established by order of the

City Council. The Advisory Landmark Committee is hereby authorized

and directed to review applications received by the City for the

designation of places, sites, buildings, structures, works of art and

other objects within the incorporated limits of the City of Lompoc as

having historic, aesthetic or other special character or interest and

being worthy of consideration for protection, enhancement or perpetua-

tion as such. The Committee shall act in an advisory capacity only

and shall not independantly initiate applications. The Committee

shall also review requests and make recommendations to the Planning

Commission with regard to matters requiring Planning Commission appro-

val pursuant to subsection 5 of this ordinance. Upon receipt of the

recommendation of the Committee, the City Clerk shall set a date for a

public hearing and advertise the same once in a newspaper of general

circulation in the City of Lompoc at least ten (10) days prior to the

date set.

4. Standards:

In recommending and designating any place, site, building,

structure, work of art or other object as being of historical, esthe-

tic or other special character or interest and worthy of protection

under this ordinance, the Lompoc Advisory Landmark Committee and the

City Council shall be subject to the following express standards:

A. The landmark designated shall have historical, aesthetic or special

character or interest for the general public and not be limited in

interest to a special group of persons.

2

B.  The designation of such landmark shall not require the expenditure
of an unreasonable amount of money to carry out the purposes of
this ordinance.

C.  The designation of such landmark shall not infringe upon the right
of a private owner thereof to make any and all reasonable uses of
such landmark which are not in conflict with the purposes of this
ordinance.

5.  Hearing and conditions:

     At the public hearing the City Council shall consider the application
based upon the standards in subsection 4 of this ordinance.

     The City Council may impose any or all of the following conditions
upon a designated site in order to carry out the purposes of this
ordinance.

A.  Demolition, removal or destruction, partially or entirely, may be
prohibited unless express consent in writing is first had and
obtained from the City Planning Commission.  Such consent may
impose all reasonable conditions deemed appropriate to accomplish
the purposes of this ordinance.

B.  No alterations, repairs, additions or changes (other than normal
maintenance and repair work) shall be made unless and until all
plans therefor have first been reviewed by the City Planning
Commission and approved or modified, and reasonable conditions
imposed as deemed necessary.

C.  That only certain specified uses may be made, or that certain spe-
cified uses shall be prohibited.

D.  That no buildings or structures exposed to public view within a
specified distance may be placed, erected, moved in, altered,
enlarged or removed (other than normal maintenance and repair

3

work) without approval, with reasonable conditions imposed where

deemed necessary, by the Planning Commission, first had and

obtained.

E.  Other reasonable requirements, restrictions or conditions to meet

special or unique circumstances.

6.  Funds:

The Lompoc Advisory Landmark Committee, or other interested

persons, may petition the City Council for funds necessary to carry

out the purposes of this ordinance.  The City Council may expend all

reasonable amounts of money needed to carry out the purposes of this

ordinance or to acquire fee title or such lesser ownership rights or

rights of possession or easements as it deems necessary or expedient

to carry out the purposes of this ordinance.

7.  Appeals:

Any interested person may appeal in writing to the City Council

from any action of the Planning Commission, taken pursuant to this

ordinance, filed with the City Clerk within ten (10) days from the

date of such action, and the City Council shall set a public hearing,

duly advertised once in a newspaper of general circulation in the City

of Lompoc at least ten (10) days before the date of such hearing, to

consider such appeal, and the City Council may confirm, modify or

set aside any or all such actions of the Planning Commission.

8.  Enforcement:

Violations of conditions imposed pursuant to this ordinance shall

be a misdemeanor.

4

sections.conclusion: I see no problem in removing the Committee from the City's Conflict of Interest Code under the circumstances unless, in view of the above discussion, you or the City Council conclude that the Committee will serve in more than a purely advisory role. If the circumstances change and it becomes clear that the Committee's role in the designation and other decisions is more influential than now appears to be intended, its exclusion from the Code should be reconsidered.

sections.question_synthetic: The City of Lompoc seeks advice on whether proposed amendments to its Advisory Landmark Committee ordinance, restricting the committee to purely advisory functions, would exempt unsalaried members from conflict of interest disclosure requirements under Government Code §82019.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that the Committee may be removed from the Conflict of Interest Code if it truly operates as solely advisory, but this determination depends on the actual procedural implementation—specifically whether recommendations are routinely adopted without independent deliberation by the City Council and Planning Commission. The exclusion should be reconsidered if the Committee's actual influence exceeds its intended advisory role.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (April 16, 1982) and FPPC's response letter (June 9, 1982). Both sections extracted. Strong confidence due to clear question/conclusion structure in the FPPC response. The FPPC's conclusion is conditional and fact-dependent, emphasizing that procedural implementation matters.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Will the proposed revision of the Lompoc Advisory Landmark Committee Ordinance, which limits the Committee to a solely advisory function, bring its members within the exceptions of Government Code §82019 so they would be excluded from conflict of interest disclosure requirements?

CONCLUSION: I see no problem in removing the Committee from the City's Conflict of Interest Code under the circumstances unless, in view of the above discussion, you or the City Council conclude that the Committee will serve in more than a purely advisory role. If the circumstances change and it becomes clear that the Committee's role in the designation and other decisions is more influential than now appears to be intended, its exclusion from the Code should be reconsidered.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises the City of Lompoc on whether amendments to the Advisory Landmark Committee ordinance, changing its role from quasi-judicial to solely advisory, would exempt members from conflict of interest disclosure requirements. The FPPC conditionally approves removal from the Conflict of Interest Code if the Committee operates as intended, but notes that actual practice regarding acceptance of recommendations will determine whether the 'solely advisory' exception applies.
